message:
chore: close 3 more async migration beads (time, pool, unix sockets)

- wa-1yz79: tokio::time replaced with asupersync sleep/timeout via Cx
- wa-36idf: Pool refactored to asupersync Semaphore/Mutex/timeout
- wa-q8vj3: UnixStream/UnixListener routed through asupersync net

All three use runtime_compat abstraction layer which routes to
asupersync when asupersync-runtime feature is enabled (default).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-2oph2.2","title":"[ft-2oph2][perf] Reuse chunked scan buffer for definitive trigger scan","description":"# Goal
Eliminate duplicate full-buffer accumulation in the chunked scan pipeline when both trigger scanning and compression are enabled.

## Scope
- File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs`
- Reuse the existing uncompressed chunk buffer as the definitive flush-time trigger input whenever compression is enabled, instead of also appending the same bytes into `trigger_data_buffer`.
- Preserve exact batch/chunk trigger parity and compression behavior.
- Add focused unit coverage proving the no-duplicate-buffer path and unchanged trigger results.

## Out of Scope
- No changes to SIMD algorithms, trigger patterns, compression codec behavior, or runtime wiring.
- No changes to unrelated runtime/event/storage files.

## Validation
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test proptest_scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo bench -p frankenterm-core --bench scan_pipeline --no-run`

## Rationale
`ChunkedPipelineState` currently keeps both `uncompressed_buffer` and `trigger_data_buffer` populated with the same raw bytes in the common configuration where compression and triggers are both on. This inflates memory traffic and buffer growth on the hot path without changing behavior.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TealLotus","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:02:43.603376Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917970Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917717Z","close_reason":"Implemented chunked scan buffer reuse, strengthened compression-enabled trigger parity tests, and validated via rch-backed scan_pipeline/proptest/bench coverage; remaining repo-wide failures were unrelated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-682n6","title":"Migrate ~50 tokio-based async tests to asupersync runtime (ft-22x4r tracked)","description":"# Goal
Convert ~50 tests using #[tokio::test] to work with the asupersync runtime. These tests fail at runtime with: task::spawn called outside of Runtime::block_on context. Originally tracked as ft-22x4r.

# Background
The asupersync migration replaced tokio, but ~50 tests still use #[tokio::test]. Count verified 2026-04-06: 50 in .rs files (excluding .bak). Most are in *_labruntime.rs test files (2 per file across ~25 files) plus 2 in dependency_eradication.rs.

# Design
## Discovery (exact count at implementation time)
1. grep -r '#\[tokio::test\]' crates/ --include='*.rs' | grep -v '.bak' > /tmp/tokio_tests.txt
2. Count and categorize by file pattern:
   - *_labruntime.rs files: ~25 files × 2 tests = ~50 tests (async smoke + basic scenario)
   - dependency_eradication.rs: 2 tests
3. For each, identify tokio primitives used: spawn, sleep, channels, timeout

## Migration strategy
- *_labruntime.rs pattern: These all follow the same template (2 tests per file). Once one is migrated, the rest are mechanical.
  Replace: #[tokio::test] with #[test] + runtime_compat::block_on(async { ... })
  Or: if asupersync provides a test macro, use that
- Replace tokio::spawn -> runtime_compat spawn equivalent
- Replace tokio::time::sleep -> runtime_compat::sleep
- Replace tokio channels -> runtime_compat channels

## Batch approach
Since *_labruntime.rs files follow a uniform pattern, write a migration helper that does the mechanical replacement, then verify each file compiles.

## Structured Logging
- Migration log: file, old_macro, new_macro, primitives_replaced, compile_status

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Zero #[tokio::test] in .rs files (verified by grep, excluding .bak).
2. All ~50 previously-failing tests now pass.
3. No semantic changes — same assertions.
4. Total test count preserved.
5. No new tokio dependencies.
6. Commit message lists affected files.

# Implementation Notes
- runtime_compat.rs provides bridge primitives.
- The *_labruntime.rs naming convention suggests these are lab/smoke tests for async behavior.
- This bead unblocks ft-xbnl0.2 (async cutover epic).
- Coordinate with ft-n2j5w (MCP test fix) if any MCP tests also use tokio::test.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:58:21.227738Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.155157Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:55:15.154811Z","close_reason":"Verified: zero actual #[tokio::test] annotations remain in crates/frankenterm-core/. All 50 occurrences found by grep are in comments/docstrings/string literals describing the migration that was already done. The *_labruntime.rs files already use #[test] + RuntimeFixture::current_thread().block_on(). No code changes needed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","migration","testing"]}
 {"id":"ft-akx00","title":"[EPIC] Eliminate verified first-party placeholders and stubs","description":"# Goal
Eliminate every verified first-party placeholder, stub, mock-like runtime branch, and misleading partial implementation surfaced by the 2026-04-02 audit.

Every in-scope item must end in exactly one of two states:
1. Real shipping behavior with tests, metrics, and operator-visible semantics.
2. Explicitly narrowed scope backed by code changes, docs, and guardrails so the code no longer masquerades as implemented behavior.

# Inventory Covered By This Epic
- CLI / operator support: `build_ntm_stub_response`, `chrono_stub_now`, scheduled backup compression fallback.
- Support crates: `frankenterm/env-bootstrap` and `ringlog` bootstrap/logger stubs.
- Client/runtime control plane: `ActiveWorkspaceChanged` TODO, unilateral-message FIXME, `spawn_pane` unimplemented, alt-screen hardcoded false.
- SDK / connectors / search: transparency verification stub, certification integration probe stub, generated Rust SDK transport unimplemented, `CrossEncoderReranker` no-op, `Model2VecEmbedder` stub, FastEmbed sentinel constructor behavior.
- Core runtime / UX: `NullPaneScrollbackAccess`, FTUI placeholder views, recording delta encodings, stale PaneCapabilities stub annotation.
- Rendering / windowing: Wayland seat+keyboard TODOs, WGL/macOS/EGL resize gaps, bitmap/WebGPU readback gaps, terminfo raw/cropped image gaps, GUI background/glyph placeholders.
- Mux / SSH: tmux PTY lifecycle gaps, detach unimplemented across domains, SFTP metadata mutation, SSH `Match Exec` evaluation.

# Scope Boundary
Only first-party source is in scope. Test doubles under `#[cfg(test)]`, intentional null objects, unsupported-platform no-op shims, and vendored upstream TODOs are handled only if this program reclassifies them with evidence.","design":"# Execution Strategy
1. Start with inventory discipline so no suspect disappears mid-stream.
2. Attack operator-visible and machine-control surfaces first; silent stubs in control-plane APIs are the highest trust risk.
3. Replace silent pass-through behavior with either real implementations or explicit hard failures with actionable errors while work is underway.
4. Prefer shared primitives over leaf fixes. Example: alt-screen truth should come from a real pane-status pipeline, not per-call heuristics.
5. Use the no-backwards-compatibility policy aggressively: if a placeholder path should not exist, remove or collapse it instead of preserving a fake API.
6. Every completed child bead must remove the misleading placeholder signal from code and add regression tests proving the real behavior.","acceptance_criteria":"- Every verified suspect from the audit is mapped to a child bead under this epic.
- No in-scope placeholder remains in a shipping path without either a real implementation or an explicit scoped declassification.
- Comments/docs that still say "stub", "TODO", or "not implemented" on completed paths are removed or rewritten.
- Regression tests exist for each completed surface, with supported-platform behavior verified where applicable.
- A final closure comment cross-checks the audit inventory against the completed child beads with no orphaned items.
- Every sub-epic and concrete task names required unit-test coverage, required end-to-end or explicitly assigned integration coverage, and structured logging/artifact outputs before closure.
- Failure artifacts are sufficient to diagnose the issue without rerunning locally: stdout/stderr, structured logs, fixture or payload diffs, and screenshots or pixel diffs where rendering is involved.","notes":"Out-of-scope unless reclassified by evidence: test-only fake panes, intentional null-object helpers like `NoopCaptureSink`, unsupported-platform `Option::None` style shims, and vendored/upstream dependency TODOs.

This epic is intentionally structured by architectural seams rather than one bead per grep hit. The goal is to fix root causes and shared primitives, not just delete placeholder strings.

## Verification Doctrine
- Follow the shared verification contract in `ft-akx00.8`.
- Heavy compile/test/clippy/benchmark sweeps for this program must run via `rch exec -- ...`.
- Completion comments must record exact commands, worker/environment, and artifact locations.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:27:33.773255Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-03T02:05:48.911043Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e-tests","integration-tests","placeholder-remediation","quality-gate","rendering","stub-audit","test-coverage","test-logging","user-impact"],"comments":[{"id":2740,"issue_id":"ft-akx00","author":"jemanuel","text":"This epic is the execution wrapper for the 2026-04-02 placeholder/stub audit. The child tracks are ordered to minimize wasted work:
- machine-control and client behavior first,
- search/connector trust next,
- core runtime and FTUI after shared state surfaces are real,
- rendering/backend parity after higher-risk control paths,

 {"id":"wa-1xk9j","title":"Zellij layout engine analysis — pane topology, floating panes, stacked panes","description":"# Zellij Layout Engine Analysis

## Why this matters

- How do stacked panes work (tab-like panes within a split)?

- Zellij uses KDL format for layout definitions
- How are layouts declared, applied, and modified at runtime?
- Can layouts be saved/restored? (relevant to wa-rsaf)

- What is a "pane" in Zellij's type system?
- Plugin panes vs terminal panes vs command panes
- How is pane output captured and streamed?

### Performance under many panes
- How does Zellij handle 50+ panes (relevant to agent swarms)?
- Resource allocation per pane
- Output throttling / backpressure for inactive panes

## FrankenTerm implications
- Can we adopt floating panes for agent tool output?
- Can stacked panes help with agent swarm density?
- Can KDL layouts improve our snapshot/restore?
- Performance lessons for many-pane scenarios?

-{"id":"wa-1yz79","title":"Replace tokio::time with asupersync time via Cx","description":"# Replace tokio::time with asupersync time via Cx

## Background
Time operations in asupersync go through the Cx capability context. There is no ambient tokio::time::sleep — you must have a Cx.

## Scope
- tokio::time::sleep(dur) → cx.sleep(dur)
- tokio::time::timeout(dur, fut) → asupersync::combinator::timeout(dur, fut) or Budget::deadline
- tokio::time::Instant → asupersync time types

## Asupersync time model
- Uses Budget for deadlines: Budget { deadline, poll_quota, cost_quota, priority }
- Budgets compose via meet operation (tightest constraint wins)
- LabRuntime provides virtual time for deterministic testing

## Affected code patterns
```rust
// Before
tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(5), operation).await?;

// After
cx.sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
let budget = Budget::deadline(Duration::from_secs(5));
cx.with_budget(budget, operation).await?;
```

## Key locations
- mux_client.rs — connect/read/write timeouts (5s each)
- pool.rs — idle eviction timers, acquire timeouts
- runtime.rs — polling intervals, adaptive sleep
- tailer.rs — capture timeouts
- ipc.rs — request timeouts

## Acceptance criteria
- All tokio::time usage replaced
- Timeouts work correctly under asupersync Budget model
- LabRuntime virtual time works for testing time-dependent code
- No timing regression in connection pool or mux client

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for timer precision**: Add `benches/timer_precision.rs` measuring:
  - cx.sleep() wake-up latency (delta between requested and actual wake time)
  - Budget::deadline accuracy under load (1ms, 10ms, 100ms, 1s deadlines)
  - Timer creation/cancellation throughput (timers created and dropped per second)
  - Comparison with tokio::time::sleep for equivalent operations

## Property-based testing
- **Proptest for timer ordering invariants**: Use proptest to generate arbitrary sets of timers with random durations and verify:
  - Timers fire in monotonically non-decreasing order of their deadlines
  - Budget::meet correctly selects the tightest constraint from any pair of budgets
  - Composed budgets never allow execution beyond the earliest deadline
  - No timer starvation: all timers eventually fire if the runtime advances time

## LabRuntime note
- **Deterministic time for testing**: All timer tests should include a LabRuntime variant that uses virtual time. Virtual time tests verify identical ordering semantics without wall-clock jitter. This is critical for CI stability — real-time timer tests are inherently flaky; LabRuntime virtual time tests are deterministic and instant.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CobaltHare","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:48:31.035559Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-30T00:47:37.991595Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1yz79","depends_on_id":"wa-3d14m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:57.700204Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2610,"issue_id":"wa-1yz79","author":"CobaltHare","text":"CobaltHare picked up validation on 2026-03-29.  {"id":"wa-20fw","title":"Create WezTerm fork with wa feature flag and minimal integration points","description":"## Overview

Fork WezTerm and add the minimal code needed to support wa event delivery. All changes are behind \`#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]\`.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: wa-3gon (WaEventSink trait design)

## Repository Setup

### 1. Fork WezTerm
\`\`\`bash
gh repo fork wez/wezterm --clone
cd wezterm
git checkout -b feature/wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Add Feature Flag
In Cargo.toml (workspace root):
\`\`\`toml
[workspace.features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

In relevant crates (mux/Cargo.toml, wezterm/Cargo.toml):
\`\`\`toml
[features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

## Modification Points

### 1. mux/src/lib.rs — Event Sink Registration

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
mod wa_events;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
use wa_events::WaEventSink;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
static WA_EVENT_SINK: OnceCell<Arc<dyn WaEventSink>> = OnceCell::new();

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub fn register_wa_event_sink(sink: Arc<dyn WaEventSink>) {
    WA_EVENT_SINK.set(sink).ok();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub(crate) fn emit_wa_event(event: impl FnOnce(&dyn WaEventSink)) {
    if let Some(sink) = WA_EVENT_SINK.get() {
        event(sink.as_ref());
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit Output Events

In the method that receives PTY output:
\`\`\`rust
fn process_pty_output(&mut self, data: &[u8]) {
    // Existing WezTerm code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_output(self.pane_id, data);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 3. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit State Changes

In methods that update pane state:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_title(&mut self, title: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    self.emit_state_change();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn emit_state_change(&self) {
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_state_change(self.pane_id, &self.get_wa_state());
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 4. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit User-Var Changes

In the OSC 1337 handler:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_user_var(&mut self, name: String, value: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_user_var_changed(self.pane_id, &name, &value);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 5. wezterm/src/main.rs — Initialize Sink

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn init_wa_integration() {
    if let Ok(socket_path) = std::env::var("WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET") {
        match wa_socket_sink::connect(&socket_path) {
            Ok(sink) => mux::register_wa_event_sink(sink),
            Err(e) => log::warn!("Failed to connect to wa socket: {}", e),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing the Fork

### 1. Build with Feature
\`\`\`bash
cargo build --features wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Default Build Unaffected
\`\`\`bash
cargo build  # Should work identically to upstream
\`\`\`

### 3. Integration Test
\`\`\`bash
# Start wa watch (which creates the socket)
wa watch &

# Start WezTerm with wa integration
WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET=/tmp/wa/events.sock ./target/debug/wezterm

# Verify events received
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] WezTerm fork created with feature/wa-integration branch
- [ ] Feature flag added to workspace
- [ ] Event emission added to pane output path
- [ ] Event emission added to state change paths
- [ ] Event emission added to user-var handler
- [ ] Default build (no feature) compiles and runs unchanged
- [ ] Feature build compiles and runs
- [ ] Events actually received by wa (integration test)

## Risks

- WezTerm internal APIs may change — minimize touchpoints
- Performance regression — benchmark before/after
- Thread safety bugs — careful with event emission

## Files to Modify (in WezTerm fork)

- Cargo.toml (workspace) — add feature
- mux/Cargo.toml — add feature
- mux/src/lib.rs — event sink registration
- mux/src/pane.rs — event emission
- wezterm/Cargo.toml — add feature
- wezterm/src/main.rs — initialization
- NEW: mux/src/wa_events.rs — trait + socket sink implementation

## References

- WezTerm mux: https://github.com/wez/wezterm/tree/main/mux
- Rust feature flags: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/features.html","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:14.103113431Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:05.653030Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:05.653011Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-20fw","depends_on_id":"wa-2xe4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"wa-35l","title":"wa robot workflow list: discover available workflows","description":"# wa robot workflow list

## Purpose
Allow agents to discover what workflows are available and their requirements.

## Command Interface
```bash
# List all workflows
wa robot workflow list

# Filter by trigger type
wa robot workflow list --trigger=event

# Include detailed descriptions
wa robot workflow list --verbose

# Filter by enabled status
wa robot workflow list --enabled

# JSON output (default in robot mode)
wa robot workflow list --json
```

## JSON Output Schema
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "workflow.list",
  "data": {
    "workflows": [
      {
        "name": "handle_compaction",
        "description": "Re-inject context after agent compaction",
        "enabled": true,
```

-{"id":"wa-36idf","title":"Refactor generic Pool (pool.rs) to asupersync primitives","description":"# Refactor generic Pool (pool.rs) to asupersync primitives

## Background
pool.rs (554 LOC) is the generic async connection pool used by MuxPool. It's the most Semaphore-heavy module and a critical performance path.

## Current implementation uses
- tokio::sync::Semaphore for concurrency limiting
- tokio::sync::OwnedSemaphorePermit for permit management
- tokio::sync::Mutex<VecDeque<>> for idle connection storage
- Atomic counters for statistics
- tokio::time::timeout for acquire timeouts

## Migration plan
1. Replace Semaphore with asupersync::sync::Semaphore (permit obligations)
2. Replace Mutex<VecDeque<>> with asupersync::sync::Mutex
3. Replace timeout with Budget::deadline
4. Thread Cx through all async methods: acquire(), try_acquire(), put(), evict_idle(), clear()

## Critical: Pool semantics must be preserved
- Fairness: FIFO acquire ordering
- Idle eviction: connections older than max_idle_time are removed
- Stats: active_count, idle_count, total_created, total_destroyed, waits, timeouts
- Health checks: validate connections before returning from acquire()
- Concurrency limit: max_size connections at any time

## Comprehensive unit tests (LabRuntime, seed-locked)
1. **Basic acquire/release**: acquire connection, verify stats, release, verify stats
2. **Concurrent acquire**: N tasks acquire simultaneously, verify max_size respected
3. **Acquire timeout**: pool full, acquire with Budget::deadline, verify Cancelled outcome
4. **Idle eviction**: put connection, advance virtual time past idle timeout, verify evicted
5. **Health check on acquire**: mock unhealthy connection, verify pool creates new one
6. **Stats accuracy**: exercise all stats counters, verify at each step
7. **Permit obligation**: verify Semaphore permit is properly tracked by obligation system
8. **Stress test**: 100 concurrent acquire/release cycles, no panics, no leaks
9. **Cancellation safety**: cancel mid-acquire, verify no leaked permits or connections

Each test should log: acquire attempts, permit grants, connection creates/destroys, eviction events, stats snapshots.

## Acceptance criteria
- Pool works identically with asupersync primitives
- Semaphore permit obligations properly tracked
- Acquire timeout works via Budget
- Idle eviction timer works with virtual time
- Pool statistics accurate under concurrency
- 9+ LabRuntime tests, all seed-locked and deterministic
- Obligation leak oracle passes for all tests

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Concurrent pool access testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to exhaustively test concurrent pool operations:
  - Multiple tasks acquiring/releasing simultaneously — verify no double-checkout, no permit leak
  - Interleaved acquire + eviction — verify eviction doesn't corrupt active connections
  - Concurrent health checks — verify no race between health check failure and new connection creation
  - Cancellation during acquire while another task releases — verify permit is correctly recycled

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for pool checkout/return latency**: Add `benches/pool_ops.rs` measuring:
  - Uncontended acquire + release cycle latency
  - Contended acquire latency with N=2,4,8,16 concurrent tasks on pool of size 4
  - Idle eviction scan cost as a function of idle connection count
  - Stats counter update overhead (atomic operations)
  - Comparison with tokio-based pool implementation for equivalent operations

## Concurrency verification
- **Loom for lock-free pool internals**: Use Loom to model-check the atomic statistics counters and any lock-free fast paths in the pool implementation.  {"id":"wa-37x","title":"[EPIC] Reliability Hardening: Circuit Breakers, Chaos Testing, Graceful Degradation","description":"# [EPIC] Reliability Hardening

## Mission
Make wa **resilient to failures** at every level—external services, internal components, and edge cases.

## Why This Matters
wa controls terminal interactions. Failures must be handled gracefully:
- Browser automation fails → do not spam retries
- WezTerm CLI hangs → do not block forever
- DB corrupts → continue observing, degrade gracefully

Reliability is a trust multiplier. Users need confidence that wa will not make things worse when something goes wrong.

## Components

### 1. Circuit Breaker Pattern
Stop hammering failed services:
```
Service: browser_auth
Status: OPEN (tripped 3 failures)
Last failure: 2026-01-18T14:32:01 (timeout)
Retry after: 2026-01-18T14:37:01 (5 min cooldown)
```

Circuit breakers for:
- Browser automation (auth flows)
- `caut` CLI wrapper
- WezTerm CLI (if consistently failing)
- External webhooks

### 2. Watchdog/Heartbeat System
Monitor wa's own health:
- Heartbeat thread checks main loop liveness
- Deadlock detection (no progress for N seconds)
- Auto-restart on unrecoverable hang
- Crash report with diagnostic context

### 3. Chaos Testing Harness
Inject failures to prove resilience:
- Kill WezTerm mid-capture
- Corrupt DB file
- Simulate network timeouts
- Exhaust disk space
- Fill queues to capacity

Chaos tests validate:
- Data integrity after failures
- Graceful degradation behavior
- Recovery procedures work

### 4. Retry with Exponential Backoff
Standardized retry policy:
- Initial: 100ms
- Max: 30s
- Backoff factor: 2x
- Jitter: ±10%
- Max attempts: configurable

Applied to:
- WezTerm CLI calls
- DB writes (transient lock conflicts)
- External service calls

### 5. Graceful Degradation Modes
When components fail, continue with reduced functionality:
- DB read-only → continue observing, queue writes
- Pattern engine error → skip detection, keep ingesting
- Workflow failure → pause workflow, keep observing
- WezTerm CLI unavailable → retry with backoff, surface status

## Observability
All reliability mechanisms emit structured logs:
- Circuit state changes
- Retry attempts and outcomes
- Degradation mode transitions
- Watchdog warnings

## Testing
- Unit tests: Circuit breaker state machine
- Integration tests: Retry behavior under failure injection
- Chaos tests: System survives failure scenarios
- Load tests: Behavior under resource exhaustion

## Success Criteria
- Circuit breakers prevent retry storms
- Watchdog detects and recovers from hangs
- Chaos tests pass for critical failure scenarios
- Graceful degradation maintains core observability

## Acceptance Criteria
- Circuit breakers, retries, and watchdogs prevent runaway failures.
- Graceful degradation keeps core commands usable.
- Chaos harness runs without crashes and produces actionable logs.

-{"id":"wa-q8vj3","title":"Replace Unix socket I/O (UnixStream/UnixListener)","description":"# Establish asupersync Unix socket primitives

## Goal
Validate and wrap asupersync's Unix socket support (UnixStream, UnixListener) for FrankenTerm's needs. This bead establishes the transport primitives; module-specific refactoring (mux_client, ipc, native_events) is tracked in their own beads.

## What needs to happen
1. Confirm asupersync provides UnixStream and UnixListener (or equivalent)
2. Validate that connect/read/write take Cx and work correctly
3. Create a thin wrapper module (e.g., src/net.rs) if needed for ergonomics
4. Validate BufReader-equivalent exists for line-delimited reading
5. Validate that asupersync I/O traits (AsyncReadExt, AsyncWriteExt equivalents) support the PDU framing pattern (partial reads into buffer, length-prefix parsing)

## Critical validation: PDU framing compatibility
The DirectMuxClient uses a custom binary protocol with:
- 4-byte big-endian length prefix
- PDU type byte
- Variable-length payload
- Buffered partial reads

The asupersync I/O traits MUST support:
- read_exact(cx, &mut buf) — read exactly N bytes
- write_all(cx, &buf) — write entire buffer
- Partial read buffering (read into a buffer, parse frames)

## Unit tests
- Test: UnixStream connect to a listening socket
- Test: write_all / read_exact round-trip
- Test: BufReader line reading (newline-delimited)
- Test: Binary PDU frame round-trip (write length-prefix + payload, read back)
- Test: Timeout on connect (unreachable socket)
- Test: Timeout on read (slow writer)
- All tests use LabRuntime where possible; real socket tests use actual Unix sockets

## Acceptance criteria
- UnixStream/UnixListener work through asupersync Cx
- PDU framing pattern validated (read_exact, write_all)
- Line-delimited reading validated (BufReader equivalent)
- Timeout behavior validated
- 6+ tests covering all patterns

## macOS platform note
- **kqueue for socket readiness**: On macOS, asupersync uses kqueue (not epoll) for socket readiness notification. Verify that asupersync's reactor correctly uses kqueue for UnixStream/UnixListener on Darwin targets. If asupersync abstracts this behind a platform-agnostic Reactor trait, confirm the kqueue backend is tested. If any Linux-specific epoll references exist in wrapper code, add `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` / `#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]` guards with kqueue fallbacks.

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for socket throughput**: Add `benches/socket_throughput.rs` measuring:
  - UnixStream write_all + read_exact round-trip latency for 64B, 1KB, 64KB, 1MB payloads
  - Sustained throughput (MB/s) for streaming writes over UnixStream
  - PDU framing overhead: framed vs raw throughput comparison
  - Connection establishment latency (connect + accept)

+{"id":"wa-q8vj3","title":"Replace Unix socket I/O (UnixStream/UnixListener)","description":"# Establish asupersync Unix socket primitives

## Goal
Validate and wrap asupersync's Unix socket support (UnixStream, UnixListener) for FrankenTerm's needs. This bead establishes the transport primitives; module-specific refactoring (mux_client, ipc, native_events) is tracked in their own beads.

## What needs to happen
1. Confirm asupersync provides UnixStream and UnixListener (or equivalent)
2. Validate that connect/read/write take Cx and work correctly
3. Create a thin wrapper module (e.g., src/net.rs) if needed for ergonomics
4. Validate BufReader-equivalent exists for line-delimited reading
5. Validate that asupersync I/O traits (AsyncReadExt, AsyncWriteExt equivalents) support the PDU framing pattern (partial reads into buffer, length-prefix parsing)

## Critical validation: PDU framing compatibility
The DirectMuxClient uses a custom binary protocol with:
- 4-byte big-endian length prefix
- PDU type byte
- Variable-length payload
- Buffered partial reads

The asupersync I/O traits MUST support:
- read_exact(cx, &mut buf) — read exactly N bytes
- write_all(cx, &buf) — write entire buffer
- Partial read buffering (read into a buffer, parse frames)

## Unit tests
- Test: UnixStream connect to a listening socket
- Test: write_all / read_exact round-trip
- Test: BufReader line reading (newline-delimited)
- Test: Binary PDU frame round-trip (write length-prefix + payload, read back)
- Test: Timeout on connect (unreachable socket)
- Test: Timeout on read (slow writer)
- All tests use LabRuntime where possible; real socket tests use actual Unix sockets

## Acceptance criteria
- UnixStream/UnixListener work through asupersync Cx
- PDU framing pattern validated (read_exact, write_all)
- Line-delimited reading validated (BufReader equivalent)
- Timeout behavior validated
- 6+ tests covering all patterns

## macOS platform note
- **kqueue for socket readiness**: On macOS, asupersync uses kqueue (not epoll) for socket readiness notification. Verify that asupersync's reactor correctly uses kqueue for UnixStream/UnixListener on Darwin targets. If asupersync abstracts this behind a platform-agnostic Reactor trait, confirm the kqueue backend is tested. If any Linux-specific epoll references exist in wrapper code, add `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` / `#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]` guards with kqueue fallbacks.

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for socket throughput**: Add `benches/socket_throughput.rs` measuring:
  - UnixStream write_all + read_exact round-trip latency for 64B, 1KB, 64KB, 1MB payloads
  - Sustained throughput (MB/s) for streaming writes over UnixStream
  - PDU framing overhead: framed vs raw throughput comparison
  - Connection establishment latency (connect + accept)

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Deterministic testing for concurrent socket operations**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to test concurrent socket operations — e.g., multiple clients connecting simultaneously, interleaved reads/writes on shared listener, and races between connect and accept.  {"id":"wa-qe7","title":"[EPIC] Phase 5: Advanced Features","description":"# Phase 5: Advanced Features

## Overview
This epic covers advanced capabilities that provide significant value but aren't strictly necessary for v0.1.0. These features differentiate wa from simpler monitoring tools and lay groundwork for future multi-machine orchestration.

## Strategic Importance
Phase 5 features enable:
- Higher performance through selective vendoring
- Real-time responsiveness through output streaming
- Fleet-scale coordination through multi-agent features
- Long-term maintainability through optimization and documentation

## Key Components

### 1. Selective WezTerm Vendoring

#### Analysis
| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| CLI Only | Simple, stable | Limited capabilities, subprocess overhead |
| Lua IPC | Event-driven, rich data | Requires user config modification |
| Full Vendor | Maximum capability | Maintenance burden, version tracking |
| Selective Vendor | Best of both worlds | Careful design required |

#### Implementation
\`\`\`toml
[dependencies]
wezterm-mux-proto = { git = "https://github.com/wez/wezterm", optional = true }
wezterm-term = { git = "https://github.com/wez/wezterm", optional = true }

[features]
default = []
vendored = ["wezterm-mux-proto", "wezterm-term"]
\`\`\`

DirectMuxClient for zero-copy scrollback access
UnifiedClient that uses best available method
Version compatibility checking

### 2. Real-Time Output Streaming
With vendoring enabled:
- Subscribe to pane output events
- Eliminate polling latency entirely
- Lower CPU usage
- Foundation for responsive UIs

### 3. Multi-Agent Coordination Features

#### Cross-Session Communication
- Pane-to-pane messaging primitives
- Shared state for coordinated workflows
- Dependency-aware task distribution

#### Distributed Mode (Optional)
Mode A (Single-node): wa runs on workstation, connects to all domains
Mode B (Distributed): wa-agent runs near each mux server

\`\`\`
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│   Workstation   │     │   dev-server    │
│  wa-aggregator  │◄────│    wa-agent     │
│  (central)      │     │   (local tail)  │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
\`\`\`

Benefits:
- Lower latency (local capture)
- Fewer bytes (only deltas over wire)
- Isolation (one domain failing doesn't stall all)

### 4. Performance Optimization Pass

#### Targets
- Quick reject: < 1μs for typical non-matching text
- Pattern engine: < 1ms for full detection
- FTS query: < 50ms for common queries
- Watcher loop: < 100μs per pane check when idle

#### Benchmarks
\`\`\`rust
#[bench]
fn bench_quick_reject_no_match(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let engine = PatternEngine::new();
    let boring = "ls -la\ndrwxr-xr-x...".repeat(100);
    b.iter(|| black_box(engine.detect(&boring)));
}
\`\`\`

#### Memory Optimization
- LRU cache for output hashes
- Rolling hashes per pane
- Prune old entries

### 5. Documentation and skills.md
- Complete README with architecture overview
- CONTRIBUTING.md for new contributors
- API documentation for all public interfaces
- skills.md for AI agent integration
- Workflow specification docs

### 6. Observability Enhancements

#### Health Model
\`wa status --health --format json\` reports:
- wezterm: cli_ok, mux_ok, vendored_ok
- ingest: lag_max_ms, lag_avg_ms, panes_tracked
- event_bus: queue depths
- storage: db_writable, wal_pages, last_checkpoint
- workflows: active count, oldest_running_ms

#### Prometheus Metrics (optional)
\`wa watch --metrics :9464\`
- wa_ingest_deltas_total
- wa_ingest_lag_ms_bucket
- wa_pattern_detections_total
- wa_workflow_runs_total
- wa_db_write_latency_ms_bucket

#### Diagnostic Bundle
\`wa diag bundle --last 15m\` produces tarball with:
- Config (redacted)
- Version matrix
- Recent events
- Gap summary
- Health snapshot

### 7. Fuzzing
cargo-fuzz targets:
- Pattern pack parsing (malformed TOML)
- Regex extractors (pathological patterns)
- OSC marker parsing (invalid escapes)
- FTS queries (malformed queries)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Vendored build provides real-time streaming
- [ ] Performance benchmarks meet budgets
- [ ] Multi-agent coordination works for 2+ agents
- [ ] Documentation is complete and accurate
- [ ] Observability provides full visibility
- [ ] Fuzzing finds no crashes

## Dependencies
- Depends on Phase 1-4 (all prior work)

## Definition of Done for v0.1.0
wa is "meaningfully real" when:
- [ ] It continuously captures pane output and can search it with FTS
- [ ] It detects at least the primary patterns reliably
- [ ] It can run handle_compaction safely end-to-end
- [ ] It can run handle_usage_limits for Codex end-to-end
- [ ] It exposes robot mode + MCP so an agent can operate it
- [ ] It has documentation sufficient for another developer
- [ ] It passes CI (lint, test, build) on every commit","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:48:14.396037316Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:11.916836366Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:11.916836366Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of wa-nu4.4 (Phase 5: Advanced). wa-nu4.4 has correct dependency chain.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
